text: The surgeon initiates dissection of the preperitoneal space lateral to the semilunaris line, using robotic instruments to carefully separate tissue layers.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal space and begins forming a flap that includes the transversus abdominis fascia, similar to a transversus abdominis release, while keeping the transversus muscle intact and visibly clean.
The surgeon focuses on the hernia sac, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully reduce the hernia sac.
The surgeon enters the hernia sac sharply and dissects it free from its lateral attachments, carefully separating the sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects down to the posterior edge of the hernia sac, continuing the reduction process within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon observes the small bowel protruding through a defect in the peritoneum, indicating a herniation.
The surgeon performs photodissection to detach the external oblique fascia, entering the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon continues careful dissection until the psoas muscle and adjacent nerves are clearly visible.
The surgeon begins closing the visible defect in the peritoneum, using sutures to approximate the tissue edges.
The surgeon performs placation of the eventrated external oblique muscle, visually reinforcing the area to close the defect.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable barb suture to placate and close the space, making multiple throws to secure the closure of the defect in the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies an internal hernia of the small bowel through a mesenteric defect of the descending colon, with the bowel protruding into the retroperitoneal space.
Sharp dissection is performed during the nephrectomy, reducing the small bowel and closing the mesenteric defect of the descending colon from the peritoneal phase.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum to completely exclude the extraperitoneal space, finalizing the repair.
The surgeon measures the repair site to determine the appropriate size and shape for the mesh to be fashioned.
A 20x15 cm heavy weight polypropylene mesh is placed over the repair site, fully covering the area.
The surgeon fixes the heavy weight polypropylene mesh at cardinal points using interrupted video-micro sutures, securing it in place over the repair site.
The surgeon trims the mesh in the surgical area to prevent foldings and retraction, ensuring a smooth and secure placement over the repair site.
An additional trocar is inserted into the surgical space to facilitate the placement of a drain. The surgeon then closes the peritoneal flap using running trio barb absorbable sutures.
